title: 第 44 節
en: On the contrary, when Indra and Agni shake the ninety strongholds (III, 34, 1 ), which are said to hold the Dasapatnis as prisoners, when Indra has split the stone with the lightning, and opened the cave, he is said to have rescued the waters, the Dasapatnis. In VIII, 96, 18, we read that Indra, when 772 TNDRA, AS DELIVERER OF WOMEN. [chap.

he had killed the Vritras, let loose the hemmed-in Sindhus, and conquered the waters, the Dasapatnis. Aryapatni also is used in the sense of devapatni as in Rv. VII, 6, 5, yaA aryapatni/?, ushasaA Aakara, where Agni, a more appropriate deliverer of the Dawn than even Indra, is said to have made the Dawns once more wives of the Aryas, thus showing the close connection, or rather confusion in the minds of the Rishis of the imprisoned Dawns with the imprisoned streams. In Rv. X, 43, 8, almost the same words are used of Indra, who is said to have made the waters wives of the Aryas, yaA aryapatniA akrmot imaA apa/i.
Herakles and his Heroines.

The question now arises whether in the names of the women set free by Herakles and similar heroes in Greece we can still discover some faint traces of the names applicable to the Dawn as occurring in the Veda. We easily find among the wives of Herakles significant names, such as Auge (sunlight), Xanthis (yellow), Chryseis (golden), Iole (violet), Aglaia (resplendent), and Eone, which cannot be separated from Eos, dawn. There are besides, Prokris, dew {jrpai^), Panope, all-seeing, and a number of names beginning with evpv, wide, an epithet much cherished with reference to the wide-spreading dawn, such as Eurybia, Euryke (uruM)1, Eurypyle, Eurytele, &c.
Are all these names, particularly those beginning with Eury, to be ascribed to accident ? We saw 1 See Chips, iv, 107, 570.

Vl] HERAKLES AND HIS HEROINES. 773 how the Dawn was represented in the Veda as rich in horses, nay as herself a horse, as rich in cows, nay as herself a cow. Is it not strange then to find among the wives of Herakles not only Hippos, hut Nikippe, Klytippe, Pyrippe,Lysippe, Hippokrate, &c. ?

Of cows there are fewer traces in the names of the wives of Herakles, unless we take Euhoia in the sense of rich in cows (gomati). In the case of Theseus, when acting the same part as Herakles, we find Phaidra, the brilliant, as his wife, and she was the sister of Aria-dne (Aria-gne).

After all this, the question arises whether we are not justified in seeing in Deianeira, who was carried off by Nessos, a similar name, a name that in Sanskrit might be represented as Dasya-nari, meaning Dasa-patni, a woman, i. e. the Dawn, that has been carried off by a Dasa, and not, as the Greeks thought, a name referring to the legend of the death of Herakles. If so, we should see once more how besides the common background of Aryan mythology, there are here and there small survivals from a secondary stage also, such as the peculiar nomenclature of devapatni and dasapatni, applied to the Dawn and to the streams of rain, according as they are in the power of the devas, the bright gods, or the Dasas, the demons of darkness. I know, of course, that modern Euhemerists will laugh at all this, and consider it labour lost, for why, they ask, should we not accept facts as facts ? Why should not a prince of Argos called Herakles have had a wife called Deianeira, and friends such as Iole (violet), Xanthis (yellow), Chryseis (golden), Eone (dawn-like) ; and if the son of Deianeira is called Hyllos (surya), i. e. sunlike, the son of Eurypyle, Leukippos (white 774 HERAKLES AND HIS HEROINES. [chap.

horse), the son of Toxikrate, Lykios (luminous), is not the world full of such accidents ? I confess that I feel quite unable to argue against this, and I doubt whether any other student of mythology would even enter on such a controversy. Whoever is accustomed to the language of mythology, knows what such names mean, he expects them, and he finds them exactly as he expects them. The mere lookeron, if he sees a number of flints, some carefully shaped, others roughly shaped, others not shaped at all, but simply crushed by pressure, will often ask how it is possible to distinguish natural from artificial flints ; and when the geologist shows him the small bulge in the artificial flint caused by repeated blows of the workman, he will hardly appreciate this fact, so pregnant with enormous consequences, or accept so faint a sign as proving what in the eyes of the expert it does prove. It is the same in mythology. The occurrence of a few names with a clear intention is enough to convince the true student that he is exploring a mythological stratum, and not a Directory of the ancient towns of Argos and Mykenai, while to the outsider all this is mere nebulous fancy.
Dawn. Fors.

Having had to follow the Dawn in her various relations to Indra as the god of light, it may not be out of place to add a few words on the same goddess, as she appears in her relation to mankind. We can well understand that everything said of her in the Vedic hymns should be to her praise, for she is welcome to all except, as we saw, to evil-doers, whom in the Vi] DAWN. FORS. 775 shape of Sarawyu, Erinys, or the earliest Dawn, she finds out : and brings to light and punishment. We saw before how Ushas, the Dawn, the mother of the cows, the leader of the days 2, opens the darkness, the stable of the cows, the gates of the rock, and how the cows come forth, bringing light and gladness to the whole world. But in her more personal character Ushas, the daughter of Dyaus, the sister of Varuna and Bhaga, the wife of Surya (VII, 75, 5), appears as a beautiful woman, dancing and smiling, waking men to do their work, giving them perception and vigour. She is supposed to be rich in golden treasure, and to show her riches to her worshippers (I, 113, 4), nay to lighten up the whole world with the eye of the sun (I, 113, 9). She is in fact rich in gifts, a real Fortuna, and far more than a mere Tyche, or chance. The Romans, of course, accepted Fors and Fortuna, as one who brings (ferre), though a moment's reflection would have shown them that ferre means auferre quite as much as afferre, so that even fur and <f>a>p, thief, have been traced back to it (K. Z., xxxi, p. 462). If Fors in the sense of luck had been derived from ferre, she would be one of those abstract goddesses, familiar to us in Roman mythology, such as Cuba, Cunina, Rumina 3, Statana, or again, Virtus, Victoria, Pudicitia, &c. But if we examine these Roman goddesses or genii more carefully, we shall find that 1 Ev. VII, 75, 1: Apa druha/i tama/i ava/i a#ush/arn, 'She revealed the unlovely darkness of the enemy. '
2 Rv. VII, 77, 2 : Gavani mata, netri ahnam, 'Mother of the cows, leader of days.'
3 Cuninae propter cunas, Ruminae propter rumam, id est prisco vocabulo mammam, Varro apud Nonium, p. 1 6 7. (See p. 1 5 1 . )

many, though not all of them, were originally epithets grafted on more substantial deities. Augustinus (vii, 1 1) tells us that Ruminus was a name of Jupiter ; why should not Rumina have been a name of Juno or even of Venus, with whom Juno shares other epithets in common, such as Yictrix, Genetrix, Postvota? Fors also has such epithets as Genetrix, Victrix, Postvota, Muliebris, Mascula, and Primigenia, and has therefore a right to be considered as a substantial deity, quite as substantial as Juno or Venus, and different from such unsubstantial concepts as Ossifraga, Statana,Vaticanus, or Fabulinus.

These smaller gods and goddesses, if indeed they can be called by that name, and not rather spirits, genii or haltias, seem to be a distinguishing element of Roman mythology, but they existed in other mythologies also, though they have not been so fully preserved to us as those of the Romans. They deserve a special study as throwing light on one of the many sources of mythology, but they should all the more carefully be kept distinct from the class of Devas or Dii majorum gentium who represent another stratum of mythological thought. The question of priority is useless, for we have no means of answering it, and mere assertions would only do mischief. These smaller gods and goddesses were probably most cherished by the smaller people. It is doubtful whether they ever possessed temples or were represented by statues, but like the Lares, they probably were far better known among the servants and in the nurseries than Mars and Minerva.

If now we examine the nature of Fors more closely we shall find that she certainly was not a mere epithet, nor a mere haltia, but one of the oldest Vi] DAWN. FORS. 777 goddesses worshipped in Italy, a being of flesh and blood, not a mere abstraction, a fairy or bogie.

It has been doubted whether Ushas, the Dawn, was conceived in the Veda as a kind of Fortuna, but the passages quoted above are sufficient to show that she was supposed to possess treasures, and that these treasures were meant for her worshippers (Rv. IV, 51, 7; VII, 41, 2). This character of Ushas became still more prominent in the later literature, as we may see, for instance, in the Adbhuta-brahmawa1 of the Sama-veda; There we read (§ 101) :—

If the Dawn (ushasi) does not rise, let him sacrifice ; May the beauty of the ordering Dawn rise up, May the pale traveller make up his bundle and go ! May the wise Dawn who brings the manifold seasons, Order what is to come to me !

Can we find a clearer Fortuna rising from the light of the Dawn ? Can we have a better Fortuna than a Dawn who is rich in treasures, and who orders all that is to come to her worshippers ? These facts at all events should not be doubted, but gratefully accepted as a thread to guide us through the labyrinth of mythology.

To those who think that the Romans must be the best judges of their gods, the popular idea that Fors is Dea quae fert, would no doubt carry great weight. We might even refer to the German ' Was sich zutriigt,' now called Zufall, in Icelandic at-burdr. But scholars who are aware of the etymological crudities of which both Greeks and Romans were guilty in their etymological explanations of the names of the gods, will no longer allow themselves to be influenced by Roman folk-etymologies. They will 1 See Weber, Omina und Portenta, p. 364.

rather look to the old worship and the old customs which surrounded the myth of Fortuna. Now this goddess Fortuna was worshipped at Praeneste, not like a shadowy Rumina, but like Ushas as Divo duhita, the daughter of Jupiter, though at the same time as holding Jupiter and Juno on her lap and giving the breast to young Jupiter. This very incongruity at once tells that we are moving in ancient mythology, and reminds us of the Dawn who in the Veda is called not only the daughter of Dyaus, but at the same time the mother of all the gods (Rv. I, 113, 19). It should be remembered also that the Fortuna Virgo had her festival on the same day as the Mater Matuta, the mother of the morning, and that Fors was actually with Pales and Ceres one of the Roman Penates.

In an old inscription she is spoken of as Fortunai Diovos fileiai primoceniai. This Primogenia or Primigenia may again be matched by the Dawn being invoked in the Veda as the first (prathama) at every morning sacrifice. She is also called agriya, the foremost goddess. Thus Eos is called r]piyevrj<;, possibly Apollon also. Erigone, again, seems to have been a name of the Dawn. It is curious that she should be called Aletis, the wanderer, like Sarama, &c, and that Aletes should be the name of a son of Ikarios and of Aigisthos, just as Erigene or Erigone is the name of a daughter of Ikarios and of Aigisthos. Here, however, there remains much to be cleared up. There are few things that are said of the Roman Fortuna that could not be matched by parallel sayings about Ushas, the Dawn \ 1 Biographies of Words, 'Fors Fortuna,' pp. 1-16.

Vl] DAWN. FORS. 779 The question therefore that has to be answered is whether Fors or Fortuna could in her origin have meant the Dawn. I may have been right or wrong in my derivation of Fors, but my arguments deserved at all events careful examination. Nothing is gained by mere assertions, even if they emanate from the highest authorities. When I said that Fors could not be derived from ferre, every scholar must have seen that what I meant was that, as a matter of fact, no case has hitherto been brought forward where any of the derivatives of the root bhar exhibit the deep-toned o, and that fordus forms no exception. That the deep-toned o was possible, has never been denied, and those who follow Noreen would probably go much further. The question was not whether such an o was possible, but whether it was real. By proving the unreality of o in the Latin derivatives of ferre l, I thought I had done all that could be expected. Then, after I had proved not only the possibility, but the reality of the deeptoned o in derivatives of the root ghar and therefore the perfect regularity of the equation Forti = Dawn, the question had ceased to be linguistic, and had become purely mythological. And here I certainly was not prepared to be asked whether I thought that forte evenit meant ' it happened by 1 I quoted Brugmann, § 172, 3, where he shows that e may in Latin be represented by e and i, but only under very special circumstances by 6. I quoted De Saussure, who says (p. 79) : ' Le latin, fort chiche de ces a, en met parfois oil il n'en faut point. II a les neutres pondes de pend et foedes de feid, alors que la regie constante des themes en as est de garder a dans la racine.'
VOL. II. A a 780 DAWN. FORS. [chap.

the intervention of the Dawn.' We might as well ask whether journalist meant ' a son of heaven or Dyaus.' We must not play with questions which to a scholar are serious. The result of this long and to me painful controversy may therefore be summed up as I summed it up in the Academy1.

Is the derivation of Fors from GHAR etymologically correct ? Is the conception of Fors as Dawn mythologically correct ? Is there besides Fors any other derivative from BHAR which shows o ? But the most important question of all is this, Is there any other ancient Roman deity like Fors, meaning no more than the carrier, and yet called the daughter of Jupiter, and the nurse of Jupiter and Juno? How well has Kuhn (K. Z., xvi, p. 173) expressed his appreciation of the true character of Fors Fortuna, when he writes : ' Es zeigt sich bei allem, was an den Dienst dieser Gottin geknlipft ist, eine so eigenthumlich Romische und tief angelegte Auffassung, dass sie gewiss nicht anders als aus dem eigensten Volksbewusstsein heraus erwachsen sein kann.'

These are very simple questions that can be answered in the negative or the affirmative by any scholar, and can surely be answered sine ira et studio. In trying to answer such questions as whether Fors was derived from ferre or from the same root that yielded formus, our language might surely be serious and scholarly, even if it cannot be courteous.
Agni.
Many people are still under the impression that Comparative Mythology is built up entirely on Com- 1 Academy, March 3, 1888.

Vi] AGN1. 781 parative Philology, and that it appeals exclusively to etymology in support of its views. Nothing can be more mistaken. Comparative Mythology could exist and light up more or less the darkest corners of mythology in every part of the world, not only in India or Greece, but in Africa and America also, if not a single name of any god or hero had been preserved or could be analysed etymologically or could be compared with cognate names in cognate languages.
zh: 相反地，當因陀羅與阿格尼搖撼九十座堡壘時（III, 34, 1），據說這些堡壘囚禁著達薩帕特尼斯，當因陀羅用閃電擊開石頭，開啟了山洞，他便被說成拯救了眾水、達薩帕特尼斯。在 VIII, 96, 18 中，我們讀到因陀羅——772 因陀羅為女性的解救者 [chap.

他已殺死了弗栗多（Vritras），解放了被堵截的辛度河（Sindhus），征服了水域──達薩帕特尼（Dasapatnis）。雅利安帕特尼（Aryapatni）也被用作提婆帕特尼（devapatni）的同義詞，如見於《梨俱吠陀》第七卷第六讚第五節，其中阿耆尼被稱為一位比因陀羅更為恰當的晨曦送行者，據稱他曾使晨曦再次成為雅利安人的妻子（yaA aryapatni/?，ushasaA Aakara），這展現了吠陀聖賢心中被囚禁的晨曦與被囚禁的河流之間的密切關聯，或毋寧說是混淆。在《梨俱吠陀》第十卷第四十三讚第八節，幾乎相同的措辭被用於因陀羅，據稱他使水域成為雅利安人的妻子（yaA aryapatniA akrmot imaA apa/?）。
赫拉克勒斯及其女英雄

現在產生了一個問題：我們是否能在赫拉克勒斯（Herakles）及希臘類似英雄解救的女性名字中，仍然發現某些微弱的痕跡，與吠陀中適用於曙光的名字相呼應。我們可以輕易地在赫拉克勒斯的妻子們的名字中發現一些重要的含義，例如奧格（Auge，陽光）、克珊西斯（Xanthis，黃色）、克律塞伊斯（Chryseis，金色的）、埃俄勒（Iole，紫羅蘭色）、阿格萊亞（Aglaia，光輝璀璨的），以及埃俄涅（Eone），這不能與厄俄斯（Eos，曙光）分開。此外還有普羅克里斯（Prokris，露水 πρόσαι）、帕諾普（Panope，全知者），以及許多以εὐρυ（寬廣）開頭的名字，這是對廣闊遠展的曙光所偏愛的表述，例如歐律比亞（Eurybia）、歐律克（Euryke，urus）、歐律庇勒（Eurypyle）、歐律忒勒（Eurytele）等。
這些名字，特別是那些以 Eury 開頭的名字，是否應當歸諸於偶然？我們已經看到 ¹ 參見 Chips, iv, 107, 570。
VI] 赫拉克勒斯及其女性伴侶 773 吠陀中曾將曙光描繪為富有馬匹，甚至將其本身視為馬，富有牛群，甚至將其本身視為牛。那麼在赫拉克勒斯的妻子中發現不僅有希波斯（Hippos），還有尼基佩（Nikippe）、克呂提佩（Klytippe）、皮里佩（Pyrippe）、呂西佩（Lysippe）、希波克拉特（Hippokrate）等，豈不奇異乎？
在赫拉克勒斯妻子的名字中，牛的痕跡較少，除非我們將歐厄婭（Euhoia）理解為富有牛隻之意（gomati）。在忒修斯的情況下，當他扮演與赫拉克勒斯相同的角色時，我們發現他的妻子是光輝者斐德拉（Phaidra），而她是阿莉亞德涅（Aria-dne, Aria-gne）的妹妹。

在所有這些論述之後，一個問題隨之而起：我們是否有理由在被涅索斯掠走的狄亞涅拉身上看到一個相似的名字，一個在梵文中可能表示為 Dasya-nari 的名字，其意為 Dasa-patni，即一位女性——黎明女神，她被一位提婆（Dasa）掠走，而非如希臘人所認為的那樣，是一個指涉海剌克勒斯之死的傳說的名字呢？若是如此，我們應該再次看到，除了雅利安神話的共同背景外，這裡那裡還有來自次級階段的小規模遺存，例如應用於黎明女神和雨流的奇特命名法——devapatni 和 dasapatni，取決於它們是處於提婆（明亮的眾神）的權力之下，還是處於提婆（黑暗的惡魔）的權力之下。當然，我很清楚現代的歷史化主義者將會對所有這一切嘲笑不已，視之為徒勞之舉，因為他們問道，我們為何不應該接受事實就是事實呢？為什麼阿爾戈斯的一位王子名叫海剌克勒斯，卻不能有一位名叫狄亞涅拉的妻子，以及諸如艾俄勒（紫羅蘭）、克桑西絲（黃色）、克律塞伊絲（金色）、厄俄涅（黎明般的）這樣的朋友呢？而且，如果狄亞涅拉的兒子名叫許洛斯（Hyllos）（蘇利耶），即太陽般的，歐律皮勒的兒子名叫盧基普斯（白色），

馬），托克西克拉特斯之子，呂基奧斯（luminous，發光者），難道世界不是充滿了這樣的巧合嗎？我承認我覺得完全無法反駁這一點，我也懷疑是否有任何其他神話學研究者甚至願意進入這樣的爭論。凡是熟悉神話語言的人都知道這些名字意味著什麼，他期待它們，並且他發現它們正好如他所期待的那樣。單純的旁觀者，如果他看到許多燧石，有的經過精心打製，有的粗糙打製，有的根本沒有打製，只是被壓力壓碎，他常常會問如何可能區分天然燧石和人工燧石；當地質學家向他展示人工燧石上由工人反覆敲擊而造成的微小凸起時，他幾乎不會領悟這一事實的重大意義，或接受這樣微弱的跡象如同它在專家眼中所證明的那樣。神話學亦然。只要出現少數幾個具有明確意圖的名字，就足以讓真正的研究者確信他正在探索的是一個神話層次，而非古代阿爾戈斯和邁錫尼城鎮的名錄，然而在局外人眼中，這一切不過是虛無縹緲的幻想。
晨曦。命運。

既然我們已經追隨晨曦（Ushas）在她與光明之神因陀羅的各種關係中的蹤跡，不妨再加上幾句關於同一位女神的話語，如她與人類的關係所呈現的樣子。我們很容易理解，吠陀讚歌中對她所說的一切都是頌揚之詞，因為她受到所有人的歡迎，除了如我們所見的惡行者，她以薩拉維尤（Sarawyu）、厄里倪斯（Erinys）或最古老晨曦的形象，將他們找出來並帶至光明和懲罰。我們前面已經看到，身為牛群之母、日子的領導者的烏沙斯（Ushas）晨曦，如何開啟黑暗、牛群的牛棚、磐石的門戶，以及牛群如何現身，為整個世界帶來光明和喜樂。但在她更具個人特徵的角色上，烏沙斯，特尤斯的女兒、伐樓那（Varuna）和婆迦（Bhaga）的妹妹、蘇利耶（Surya）的妻子（VII, 75, 5），呈現為一位美麗的女性，舞蹈著、微笑著，喚醒人類去從事他們的工作，賦予他們知覺和活力。她被認為富有黃金寶藏，並向她的膜拜者展示她的財富（I, 113, 4），更有甚者，她以太陽之眼照亮整個世界（I, 113, 9）。事實上她充滿禮物恩賜，真正的幸運女神，遠勝於單純的泰凱（Tyche）或機運。羅馬人當然接納了福爾斯和福爾圖娜（Fors and Fortuna），作為一位帶來者（ferre），儘管稍加思考就會顯示，ferre 既意味著 auferre 也意味著 afferre，因此甚至盜賊（fur）和小偷（φώρ）也被追溯回這個詞根（K. Z., xxxi, p. 462）。若福爾斯（Fors）在幸運的意義上源自 ferre，她將是我們在羅馬神話中熟悉的那類抽象女神之一，如庫巴（Cuba）、庫妮娜（Cunina）、盧米娜（Rumina）、斯塔塔娜（Statana），或者維爾圖斯（Virtus）、維多利亞（Victoria）、普迪奇提亞（Pudicitia）等等。但若我們更仔細地檢視這些羅馬女神或精靈，我們將會發現：
2 《梨俱吠陀》第七卷第七十七讚歌第二頌：Gavani mata, netri ahnam，意為「牛群之母，日子的引領者」。
3 「Cuninae 之於 cunas，Ruminae 之於 rumam，即以古老詞彙言之 mammam」——瓦羅引自農提烏斯，第 167 頁。（參見第 151 頁。）

其中許多雖然並非全部，原本都是附著於更實質的神祇之上的修飾語。奧古斯丁（七，一一）告訴我們，魯米努斯是朱庇特的一個名字；盧米娜何以不能是朱諾甚至維納斯的名字呢？朱諾與維納斯共享其他修飾語，諸如勝利者（Victrix）、生育者（Genetrix）、應許者（Postvota）？福斯亦具有生育者（Genetrix）、勝利者（Victrix）、應許者（Postvota）、婦女者（Muliebris）、男性者（Mascula）和原始者（Primigenia）等修飾語，因此有理由被視為實質的神祇，完全如同朱諾或維納斯一樣實質，迥異於骨碎魔（Ossifraga）、司昂神（Statana）、梵蒂卡努斯或法布利努斯等虛無的概念。

這些較小的神祇，如果確實可以用此名稱稱呼它們而非精靈、天才或守護神（haltias）的話，似乎是羅馬神話中的一個區別性要素，但它們也存在於其他神話體系中，儘管對我們而言保存得並不如羅馬那些神祇那麼完整。它們值得特別研究，因為它們能夠闡明神話的眾多來源之一，但更應謹慎地將它們與提婆或更高級神祇（Dii majorum gentium）的類別區分開來，後者代表了另一層神話思想。優先性的問題是無用的，因為我們沒有辦法回答它，單純的主張只會造成危害。這些較小的神祇可能最受小民族所珍視。它們是否曾擁有廟宇或由雕像代表是令人懷疑的，但像家神（Lares）一樣，它們在僕人和托兒所中的知名度可能遠高於戰神和智慧女神。
如果我們現在更仔細地審視弗爾司（Fors）的本質，將會發現她確實不僅是一個單純的修飾詞，也不是一個單純的哈爾蒂亞（haltia），而是在義大利受祀的最古老的女神之一，是一個血肉之軀的存在，而非單純的抽象概念、精靈或妖怪。
有人質疑烏莎斯（Ushas）——曙光女神——在《吠陀》中是否被理解為某種幸運之神，但上述引述的段落足以表明，她被認為擁有財寶，而這些財寶是為了她的信仰者（梨俱吠陀 IV, 51, 7；VII, 41, 2）。烏莎斯的這一特性在後來的文獻中變得更加突出，例如我們在《娑摩吠陀》的《奇蹟梵書》（Adbhuta-brahmawa）中可以看到；在那裡我們讀到（§ 101）：——
若曙光（ushasi）未升起，彼當獻祭；願有序之曙光之美升起，願蒼白之旅人整裝而去！願智慧之曙光，帶來眾多季節者，為我安排將至之事！
我們能否在晨曦之光中發現一個更為清晰的幸運女神（Fortuna）？我們能否擁有比一位富有寶藏、為其崇拜者安排一切未來之事的晨曦更好的幸運女神？無論如何，這些事實不應被質疑，而應被感恩地接納，視之為引領我們穿過神話迷宮的線索。

對於那些認為羅馬人必定是判斷其眾神的最佳評判者的人來說，將福爾圖娜（Fors）理解為「命運女神」（Dea quae fert）的通俗觀念，無疑會產生很大的說服力。我們甚至可以參照德文「Was sich zutriigt」（現稱為 Zufall）與冰島文的 at-burdr。然而，那些瞭解希臘人和羅馬人在解釋眾神名稱時所犯之語源學粗陋謬誤的學者，將不再容許自己受到羅馬民間詞源學的影響。¹ ¹ 見韋伯著，《徵兆與奇蹟》（Omina und Portenta），第 364 頁。

寧可回溯到環繞著福爾圖娜（Fortuna）神話的古老崇拜與古老習俗。這位福爾圖娜女神在普雷涅斯特受到崇拜，不像影幽的盧米娜（Rumina），而是如同烏莎（Ushas）一樣，被稱為「木星之女」（Divo duhita），儘管同時她又抱著木星和朱諾，並以乳房哺育年幼的木星。這種明顯的矛盾本身就立即告訴我們，我們正在進入古代神話的領域，並提醒我們吠陀中的曙光女神，她不僅被稱為特尤斯之女，同時也被稱為眾神之母（梨俱吠陀 I, 113, 19）。還應當記得，處女福爾圖娜（Fortuna Virgo）的節日與晨曦之母瑪特爾‧瑪圖塔（Mater Matuta）在同一天舉行，而且福爾斯（Fors）實際上與帕萊斯（Pales）和刻瑞斯（Ceres）一起是羅馬家神（Penates）之一。

在一份古老的銘文中，她被稱作 Fortunai Diovos fileiai primoceniai。這個 Primogenia 或 Primigenia 可以與吠陀中關於黎明（Dawn）的祈禱相對應，黎明在每一場晨間祭祀中被祈求為最初者（prathama）。她也被稱為 agriya，即最前面的女神。因此，厄俄斯（Eos）被稱為 πρῴη（可能阿波羅也是如此）。厄里戈涅（Erigone）似乎也曾是黎明的一個名稱。有趣的是，她應當被稱為 Aletis，即漂泊者，就像莎羅摩（Sarama）等一樣；而 Aletes 應是伊卡洛斯（Ikarios）和埃癸斯托斯（Aigisthos）之子的名字，正如厄里革涅或厄里戈涅是伊卡洛斯和埃癸斯托斯之女的名字。然而，在此仍有許多事物有待釐清。關於羅馬的福爾圖娜（Fortuna）所言之事，幾乎沒有什麼不能在關於烏莎斯（Ushas）、黎明的相應說法中找到對應物。¹ ¹ 《詞語傳記》（Biographies of Words），〈Fors Fortuna〉，頁 1-16。

因此需要回答的問題是，福爾斯（Fors）或福爾圖娜（Fortuna）在其起源上是否可能曾指黎明。我在福爾斯的語源推導上可能正確，也可能錯誤，但我的論證無論如何值得仔細檢視。單純的斷言毫無意義，即使出自最高權威亦然。當我說福爾斯不能從 ferre 推導時，每位學者都必然看出我的意思是，實際上迄今為止還未有任何個案表明根 bhar 的任何衍生詞具有濁 o 音，而 fordus 亦不例外。濁 o 音的可能性從未遭否定，那些追隨諾瑞恩（Noreen）的人可能會走得更遠。問題並非濁 o 音是否可能存在，而是其是否真實存在。藉由證明拉丁文 ferre 衍生詞中 o 的非真實性，我認為我已完成所有應當完成的工作。其後，當我不僅證明了根 ghar 衍生詞中濁 o 音的可能性，而且證明了其真實性，從而證明了方程式 Forti = 黎明的完全規則性時，問題便不再是語言學的，而成為純粹的神話學問題。在這個領域，我實在沒有準備好被問及我是否認為 forte evenit 意謂「它憑著……發生」。我曾引用布魯格曼（Brugmann）第 172 節第 3 點，他指出在拉丁文中 e 可被表示為 e 和 i，但僅在極為特殊的情況下才能表示為 ō。我曾引用德‧索緒爾（De Saussure），他說（第 79 頁）：「拉丁文中，這類 a 甚為稀缺，有時將其置於不應置放之處。它有中性詞 pondes（來自 pend）和 foedes（來自 feid），儘管 as 主題的恆定規則是在詞根中保留 a。」
VOL. II. A a 780 黎明‧命運 [章節
曙光的介入。」我們不如問記者是否指稱「天之子或特尤斯（Dyaus）」。我們不應當戲弄那些對學者而言是嚴肅的問題。因此這場漫長且對我而言痛苦的爭議之結果，可按我在《學苑》（Academy）1 中的總結來闡述。

福爾斯（Fors）是否從 GHAR 推導而來在詞源學上正確？福爾斯作為曙光的概念在神話學上是否正確？除了福爾斯之外，是否還有其他來自 BHAR 的衍生詞顯示 o？但最重要的問題是：是否還有其他古羅馬神祇如福爾斯那般，其意義不過是「承載者」，卻被稱為朱庇特的女兒，以及朱庇特和朱諾的乳母？庫恩（Kuhn, K. Z., xvi, p. 173）在讚賞福爾斯‧福爾圖娜（Fors Fortuna）的真實性格時，表述得何其精妙，他寫道：「在這位女神的祭祀中所展現的一切，都顯示出一種如此獨特的羅馬性與深遠的理解，這種理解肯定不可能源自任何他處，而只能從人民最深層的意識中應運而生。」
這些都是非常簡單的問題，任何學者都可以用否定或肯定來回答，當然也可以不帶偏見與好惡地來回答。在試圖回答諸如福爾斯（Fors）是源自費雷（ferre）還是源自產生福爾穆斯（formus）的同一個根源這類的問題時，我們的語言儘管或許無法得體，但肯定可以是嚴肅而富學術性的。
阿格尼（Agni）
許多人仍然抱持著這樣的印象，認為比較神話學完全是建立在比較
比較神話學。781 比較語言學，並且它在支持自己的觀點時完全訴諸於詞源學。這絕對是一個誤解。比較神話學即使沒有保存任何神祇或英雄的名字、即使沒有任何名字可以從詞源上進行分析、或可以與同源語言中的同源詞進行比較，也完全可以存在，並在或多或少的程度上照亮世界上每一處神話的最幽暗角落，不僅在印度或希臘，也在非洲和美洲。
